source_intake_record.raw_question_text: An alpha particle (two protons, two neutrons) moves into a stationary gold atom (79 protons, 118 neutrons), passing through the electron region that surrounds the gold nucleus like a shell and headed directly toward the nucleus. The alpha particle slows until it momentarily stops when its center is at radial distance \( r = 9.23\ \mathrm{fm} \) from the nuclear center. Then it moves back along its incoming path. (Because the gold nucleus is much more massive than the alpha particle, we can assume the gold nucleus does not move.) Assume that the only force acting between the alpha particle and the gold nucleus is the (electrostatic) Coulomb force and treat each as a single charged particle. What was the kinetic energy \( K_i \) of the alpha particle when it was initially far away (hence external to the gold atom)?

multi_step: solving requires combining multiple facts from the text: alpha charge, gold nuclear charge, turning-point condition, and Coulomb interaction
the problem involves at least a two-step derivation: infer that initial kinetic energy equals electrostatic potential energy at closest approach, then compute the numerical value
unit handling is needed because the stopping distance is given in fm and the answer is in MeV
multiple-choice options are present, but arriving at the correct option still requires a physics calculation
verifiability: answer_text is a specific numeric result with units: 24.6 MeV
choices provide an additional discrete check target, with the stated answer matching option B
the setup is deterministic under the stated assumptions (only Coulomb force, stationary gold nucleus)
registry_passed=true, question_present=true, and answer_present=true support reliable downstream checking
